evidence_quotes.quote: the unilateral removal of purchased video games.
Lack of transparency: the consumer is often not informed, at the time of purchase, of the dependence on external servers or of the minimum service duration.

paragraphs.text: As a European citizen and consumer of digital products, I would like to draw the Commission’s attention to an aspect that I consider relevant to the Digital Fairness Act: the protection of prematurely dallobsolescence consumers and the unilateral removal of purchased video games. Today, many video games, whether single-player or multiplayer, depend on central servers or online authentication systems also for basic functions. In many cases, publishers have deactivated such servers a few years after their launch, making it impossible for those who purchased it to access the game on a regular basis. This practice results in the loss of a digital good that has already been paid, with no alternative offline use or storage methods. This scenario raises three main issues: 1. Lack of transparency: the consumer is often not informed, at the time of purchase, of the dependence on external servers or of the minimum service duration. 2. Unfair commercial practice: the sale of a product without making it clear that it may become unusable at the discretion of the publisher may be misleading, contrary to the principles of contractual fairness. 3. Cultural loss: video games are part of Europe’s cultural and creative heritage; their premature destruction damages the preservation of digital works of historical and artistic value. I propose that the Digital Fairness Act integrate specific measures to: — Pre-purchase information requirement: clearly indicate the dependency on online services, the minimum duration of access guaranteed and the conditions that may lead to deactivation. — Right to an alternative use: in the event of a disruption of service, ensure an offline mode or blurring of private servers, where technically possible and without infringing intellectual property rights. — Unjustified removal ban: prevent the deactivation of a paid digital good except for unavoidable technical reasons, subject to appropriate compensation or alternative solution. — Alignment with Directive (EU) 2019/770, extending the safeguards on the conformity of digital content to cover also the post-support phase and long-term preservation. These measures would be fully consistent with the objectives of the Digital Fairness Act, in particular: — combating deceptive practices; — strengthen the transparency of contractual terms and conditions; — reduce power loopholes between suppliers and consumers; — promote a more sustainable and trustworthy digital economy. Incorporating such rules would have a twofold benefit: protect consumers from economic losses and contribute to the preservation of video-fictitious works as part of Europe’s cultural heritage. In an increasingly digital market, user confidence also depends on the certainty that the goods purchased will not be arbitrarily diverted or rendered unusable. I therefore call on the Commission to consider the issue of the destruction of video games as an emblematic case of unfair commercial practice and lack of transparency, to be explicitly addressed in the Digital Fairness Act. Clear regulation in this sense would strengthen not only consumer rights but also the credibility of the European Digital Single Market.
